question: Table 1 Section 3 of the JORC Code covers reporting requirements for which category?

options.A: Production reconciliation
options.B: Ore Reserve estimation and reporting
options.C: Mineral Resource estimation and reporting
options.D: Exploration Results

explanation: Table 1 Section 3 specifically addresses the criteria for reporting Mineral Resources. It covers the geological model, estimation methodology, classification criteria, cut-off grades, and numerous other technical parameters that must be disclosed when publicly reporting a Mineral Resource estimate. Section 2 covers Exploration Results and Section 4 covers Ore Reserves. Understanding which section governs which reporting category is fundamental to JORC compliance.
key_concept: Table 1 Section 3 governs Mineral Resource estimation and reporting

question: What is the foundational requirement for any Mineral Resource estimate under JORC?

options.A: A completed feasibility study
options.B: A geological model that describes the interpreted geology, mineralisation controls, and geometry of the deposit
options.C: A signed mining lease
options.D: A minimum of 100 drill holes

explanation: Every Mineral Resource estimate must be underpinned by a geological model that describes the interpreted geology, including lithological controls, structural controls, mineralisation style, and deposit geometry. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of the geological interpretation and the assumptions underlying the model. The geological model determines how data is grouped into estimation domains, which in turn controls how grades are interpolated. A flawed geological model will produce unreliable resource estimates regardless of the sophistication of the estimation method used.
key_concept: A geological model is the mandatory foundation of every Mineral Resource estimate

question: Ordinary kriging is preferred over inverse distance weighting (IDW) for grade estimation in many deposits because:

options.A: Kriging produces unbiased estimates with minimum estimation variance, using the spatial correlation structure (variogram) of the data
options.B: Kriging always produces higher grade estimates than IDW
options.C: Kriging is always faster to compute
options.D: IDW is not permitted under the JORC Code

explanation: Ordinary kriging is a geostatistical method that uses the variogram (spatial correlation model) to produce the Best Linear Unbiased Estimate (BLUE) with minimum estimation variance. Unlike IDW, which uses an arbitrary distance-weighting power, kriging weights are derived from the actual spatial structure of the data, accounting for data clustering, anisotropy, and the geometry of the estimation domain. However, both methods are acceptable under JORC — the Competent Person must justify the chosen method's appropriateness for the deposit type. IDW can be acceptable for well-drilled, relatively homogeneous deposits.
key_concept: Kriging uses variogram-derived weights for minimum variance, unbiased grade estimation

question: A variogram models which property of the grade data in a deposit?

options.A: The total tonnage of the resource
options.B: The relationship between gold price and ore grade
options.C: The spatial continuity and correlation of grades as a function of distance and direction
options.D: The average grade of the deposit

explanation: A variogram (or semi-variogram) quantifies how grade similarity between sample pairs changes as a function of separation distance and direction. It captures three key parameters: the nugget (short-range variability), the sill (total variance), and the range (distance beyond which grades are uncorrelated). Anisotropic variograms capture directional differences in continuity — for example, grades may be continuous along strike for 200m but only 50m across strike. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of variogram parameters and their influence on the resource estimate.
key_concept: Variograms model spatial grade continuity as a function of distance and direction

question: Cut-off grade in a Mineral Resource estimate is defined as:

options.A: The minimum grade at which mineralised material is considered potentially economic and is included in the resource
options.B: The grade at which the mine becomes profitable
options.C: The maximum grade permitted before top-cutting is applied
options.D: The average grade of all drill intersections

explanation: The cut-off grade is the threshold grade below which mineralised material is excluded from the Mineral Resource. It must reflect 'reasonable prospects for eventual economic extraction' as required by the JORC definition of a Mineral Resource (Clause 20). The cut-off grade is influenced by commodity prices, anticipated mining and processing costs, and recovery assumptions. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of the cut-off grade used and the basis for its determination. An unrealistically low cut-off inflates the resource tonnage with sub-economic material, while an excessively high cut-off understates the resource.
key_concept: Cut-off grade defines the minimum grade for inclusion based on reasonable economic prospects

question: The three classification categories for Mineral Resources, in order of increasing geological confidence, are:

options.A: Measured, Indicated, Inferred
options.B: Probable, Possible, Proven
options.C: Preliminary, Definitive, Final
options.D: Inferred, Indicated, Measured

explanation: The JORC Code classifies Mineral Resources as Inferred (lowest confidence), Indicated, and Measured (highest confidence) based on increasing levels of geological knowledge and confidence in the estimate (Clause 21-23). Inferred Resources are estimated with insufficient confidence for application of Modifying Factors. Indicated Resources have sufficient confidence for mine planning at the pre-feasibility level. Measured Resources have the highest confidence. Note: Proven and Probable are Ore Reserve categories, not Resource categories — a common source of confusion.
key_concept: Resource classification: Inferred < Indicated < Measured (increasing geological confidence)

question: Which of the following is the MOST critical factor in classifying a resource as Measured rather than Indicated?

options.A: The resource must exceed 1 million tonnes
options.B: Drill spacing and data quality must provide sufficient confidence that the geological and grade continuity can be confirmed with certainty
options.C: The deposit must be within 5km of existing infrastructure
options.D: A feasibility study must be completed

explanation: Measured Resources require that drill spacing, data quality, and geological understanding are sufficient to confirm geological and grade continuity with a high degree of confidence (Clause 22). The distinction from Indicated is fundamentally about the confidence in the continuity of geology, grade, and physical characteristics between sample points. This is typically demonstrated through close-spaced drilling, consistent geological model, good variogram structure, and low estimation variance. There is no tonnage threshold, infrastructure requirement, or study requirement for Resource classification — these are separate considerations.
key_concept: Measured classification requires confirmed geological and grade continuity at close drill spacing

question: Bulk density (or dry bulk density) is required for Mineral Resource estimation because:

options.A: It determines the hardness of the rock for blast design
options.B: It is used to calculate the gold price
options.C: It determines the colour of the ore
options.D: It is needed to convert estimated volumes into tonnages

explanation: Mineral Resources are reported in tonnes and grade. The block model estimates grades and volumes, but tonnage = volume x bulk density. Without accurate density data, the tonnage calculation is unreliable. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of the method used to determine bulk density (e.g., immersion, calliper, gamma logging), the number and distribution of measurements, the assumed density values by rock type, and whether moisture content has been accounted for. Density can vary significantly between oxide, transitional, and fresh rock zones, making spatially distributed measurements essential.
key_concept: Bulk density converts block model volumes to reportable tonnages

question: A Competent Person uses a top-cut of 30 g/t Au for a gold resource estimate. What must be disclosed regarding this decision?

options.A: The top-cut value and the number of samples affected
options.B: Only the top-cut value itself
options.C: Nothing — top-cutting is an internal technical decision
options.D: The top-cut value, the statistical basis for its selection, and the impact on the overall resource grade and metal content

explanation: Table 1 Section 3 requires comprehensive disclosure of high-grade management strategies. This includes the top-cut value, the statistical basis for its selection (e.g., log-probability analysis, coefficient of variation analysis, histogram breaks), the number and percentage of samples affected, and importantly, the impact on the resource grade and metal content. A top-cut that reduces the resource grade by 25% is a material factor that investors need to understand. The sensitivity of the estimate to the chosen top-cut level should also be discussed, as different Competent Persons may arrive at different top-cut values from the same data.
key_concept: Top-cut disclosure must include value, statistical basis, and impact on the resource estimate

question: Block model parameters that must be reported under Table 1 Section 3 include:

options.A: Only the block grades above the cut-off
options.B: Only the total number of blocks
options.C: Block size, search parameters, minimum and maximum samples used, and the relationship between block size and drill spacing
options.D: Only the software used for estimation

explanation: Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of block dimensions (and whether sub-blocking was used), search ellipse orientations and dimensions, minimum and maximum number of composites used per block estimate, and critically, the relationship between block size and data spacing. Block size should generally be approximately half the drill spacing — blocks significantly smaller than the data spacing produce unreliable individual block estimates (though global estimates may still be acceptable). These parameters fundamentally control the smoothing and reliability of the grade estimate.
key_concept: Block model parameters including size, search criteria, and data spacing relationship must be disclosed

question: The JORC Code requires tonnages to be reported on which moisture basis?

options.A: Always wet tonnes
options.B: Moisture basis is not relevant to JORC reporting
options.C: The moisture basis must be stated, and dry tonnes are preferred for most commodities
options.D: Always dry tonnes

explanation: Table 1 Section 3 requires that the moisture basis for tonnage reporting be clearly stated. For most metallic mineral deposits, dry tonnes are the standard because the contained metal is the value driver. However, for some commodities (e.g., iron ore, coal), the moisture content is commercially significant and both wet and dry tonnages may be relevant. Reporting tonnage without specifying the moisture basis is a compliance failure because the difference between wet and dry tonnage can be 5-15% or more, particularly for oxide and clay-rich ores. This directly affects the economic value of the resource.
key_concept: Moisture basis must be stated — dry tonnes preferred for metals, with justification

question: An Inferred Mineral Resource differs from an Indicated Mineral Resource primarily in that:

options.A: Inferred Resources are based on different geological models
options.B: Inferred Resources use different estimation methods
options.C: Inferred Resources are always smaller in tonnage
options.D: Inferred Resources have lower geological confidence and cannot be directly converted to Ore Reserves

explanation: An Inferred Resource (Clause 23) is estimated with insufficient confidence in geological and grade continuity to allow the Modifying Factors to be applied with meaningful precision. As a result, Inferred Resources cannot be directly converted to Ore Reserves — only Indicated and Measured Resources can form the basis for Probable and Proved Reserves respectively. The lower confidence typically reflects wider drill spacing, less geological understanding, or limited data quality. Inferred Resources carry the implicit warning that with more data, the estimate could change substantially — upward or downward.
key_concept: Inferred Resources have insufficient confidence for Reserve conversion — they may change substantially

question: Inverse distance weighting (IDW) estimation uses a distance power (typically IDW² or IDW³). What is the effect of increasing the power?

options.A: It reduces the total tonnage of the resource
options.B: It has no effect on the grade estimate
options.C: It gives more weight to nearby samples, creating a more localised estimate that approaches nearest-neighbour at very high powers
options.D: It gives more weight to distant samples, creating a smoother estimate

explanation: In IDW, sample weights are proportional to 1/d^n where d is distance and n is the power. Higher powers give increasingly dominant weight to the nearest samples. At IDW¹, weights decrease linearly with distance (smoother, more averaged). At IDW², weights decrease with the square of distance (standard). At very high powers (IDW⁶+), the estimate converges toward the nearest-neighbour method where only the closest sample effectively determines the block grade. Table 1 Section 3 requires justification of the IDW power chosen and comparison with alternative methods to demonstrate reasonableness.
key_concept: Higher IDW power gives more weight to nearest samples, approaching nearest-neighbour

question: A gold deposit shows strong grade anisotropy — grades are continuous along strike for 150m but only 30m down dip. How should this be reflected in the resource estimate?

options.A: Use an isotropic search ellipse with a 90m radius
options.B: Use an anisotropic search ellipse and variogram model that reflects the different continuity ranges along strike and down dip
options.C: Ignore the anisotropy and use a polygonal estimation method
options.D: Reduce all grades by 50% to account for the anisotropy

explanation: Grade anisotropy — where spatial continuity differs by direction — is common in geological deposits and must be captured in both the variogram model and the search ellipse. The variogram should model the different ranges (150m along strike, 30m down dip), and the search ellipse should be oriented to match the geological continuity directions. Using an isotropic search would inappropriately weight samples from the short-range direction equally with those along the high-continuity direction, producing biased estimates. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of variogram parameters, search orientations, and their geological rationale.
key_concept: Anisotropic variograms and search ellipses must reflect directional grade continuity

question: When JORC states that a Mineral Resource must have 'reasonable prospects for eventual economic extraction,' this means:

options.A: The current commodity price must exceed the estimated production cost
options.B: The Competent Person must consider factors like cut-off grade, mining method, processing, and access that suggest the material could realistically be mined in the future
options.C: An Ore Reserve must be simultaneously declared
options.D: A positive NPV feasibility study must be completed

explanation: The 'reasonable prospects for eventual economic extraction' test (Clause 20) requires the Competent Person to consider, at a broad conceptual level, whether the mineralisation could realistically be mined. This includes consideration of likely mining method (open pit vs underground), reasonable cut-off grades, processing characteristics, location and access, and commodity price outlook. It does NOT require a feasibility study, positive NPV, or simultaneous Reserve declaration. However, it does exclude mineralised material that has no conceivable path to economic extraction — for example, deep, low-grade material in a remote location with no infrastructure.
key_concept: Reasonable economic prospects require conceptual-level consideration of mining viability, not a feasibility study

question: A company reports a Mineral Resource that is described as 'additional to' Ore Reserves. What does this mean?

options.A: The Resource has been converted to Reserves and then additional material was found
options.B: The Resource was estimated by a different Competent Person than the Reserve
options.C: The Resource is reported after the Reserve was depleted by mining
options.D: The reported Resource tonnage does NOT include the material already converted to Ore Reserves — the Resource and Reserve are mutually exclusive quantities

explanation: When a Mineral Resource is reported as 'additional to' Ore Reserves, it means the Resource figure excludes the material that has been converted to Reserves. The total mineralised inventory = Resource + Reserve. Alternatively, a Resource can be reported as 'inclusive of' Reserves, meaning the Reserve is a subset of the reported Resource figure. The JORC Code (Clause 24) requires clear statement of which convention is used, as confusion between 'inclusive' and 'additional' can lead to double-counting or misunderstanding of the total mineral inventory. Industry practice varies by jurisdiction.
key_concept: 'Additional to' means Resources exclude Reserve material; 'inclusive of' means Reserves are a subset

question: The polygonal estimation method assigns grades to blocks based on:

options.A: A weighted average of all samples within a search ellipse
options.B: The arithmetic mean of all samples in the deposit
options.C: A geostatistical simulation of possible grade distributions
options.D: The grade of the nearest sample, with each sample assigned an area of influence using Thiessen polygons

explanation: The polygonal (Thiessen/Voronoi) method assigns each sample a polygon of influence based on the perpendicular bisectors between adjacent sample points. All blocks within a polygon receive the grade of its central sample. This is the simplest estimation method and produces locally unsmoothed estimates. It is generally only appropriate for evenly-spaced data or as a global check on more sophisticated methods. For irregularly spaced data, polygonal estimation produces highly biased results because clustered samples get small polygons while isolated samples get disproportionately large ones. Table 1 Section 3 requires justification of the chosen estimation method.
key_concept: Polygonal estimation assigns nearest-sample grades via Thiessen polygons — simple but sensitive to data spacing

question: A resource estimate is reconciled against production data from an operating mine. The reconciliation shows the model consistently overstates grade by 15%. Under JORC, what should the Competent Person do?

options.A: Withdraw the resource estimate entirely
options.B: Ignore the discrepancy as reconciliation is only informational
options.C: Automatically reduce all resource grades by 15%
options.D: Investigate the cause of the discrepancy and disclose the reconciliation results, adjustments made, or reasons if no adjustment was applied

explanation: Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of reconciliation results where applicable. A systematic 15% grade overstatement is material and demands investigation — potential causes include assay bias, selective mining, ore loss, dilution, sample support effects, or estimation bias (smoothing). The Competent Person must disclose the reconciliation results and either adjust the estimate or explain why no adjustment was made. Simply applying a blanket 15% reduction without understanding the cause could be equally inappropriate. Reconciliation is one of the most powerful validation tools for resource estimates.
key_concept: Production reconciliation discrepancies must be investigated, disclosed, and addressed

question: The 'nugget effect' in variography represents:

options.A: The effect of gold price fluctuations on resource value
options.B: The presence of visible gold nuggets in drill core
options.C: The random, short-range variability in grades that cannot be resolved by the sampling spacing, plus measurement error
options.D: The increase in grade variance at long distances

explanation: In variography, the nugget effect (C₀) is the y-intercept of the variogram — the variance that exists at zero separation distance. It represents two components: (1) micro-scale geological variability at distances shorter than the closest sample spacing, and (2) measurement error from sampling and assaying. A high nugget effect means grades are poorly correlated even at short distances, which increases estimation uncertainty and typically leads to more conservative resource classification. The term originates from gold deposits where coarse gold particles create extreme short-range grade variability, but it applies to all variogram models.
key_concept: Nugget effect = short-range variability + measurement error, visible as variogram y-intercept

question: A Competent Person is classifying blocks in a resource model. Which approach to classification is most consistent with JORC requirements?

options.A: Use a combination of data density, estimation quality metrics (kriging variance, slope of regression), geological confidence, and reconciliation data
options.B: Classify solely based on drill hole spacing with fixed distance thresholds
options.C: Allow the mining company's board to determine classification
options.D: Classify all blocks as Measured to maximise the reported resource

explanation: JORC (Clauses 21-23) requires that classification reflect the Competent Person's confidence in the geological and grade continuity, data quality, and estimation reliability. Best practice uses multiple criteria: drill spacing and data density, estimation quality metrics (kriging variance, number of informing samples, slope of regression), geological model confidence, data quality (QA/QC results), and reconciliation where available. Using drill spacing alone is overly simplistic — it does not account for geological complexity or estimation performance. Classification is the Competent Person's professional judgement and cannot be delegated to management.
key_concept: Resource classification uses multiple confidence criteria, not drill spacing alone

question: Which of the following bulk density determination methods would be LEAST reliable for a porous, oxide gold ore?

options.A: Calliper method on regular-geometry core samples
options.B: Downhole gamma-gamma density logging
options.C: Water immersion method on uncoated core samples
options.D: Water immersion method on wax-coated core samples

explanation: Water immersion on uncoated samples is unreliable for porous materials because water enters the pore spaces during measurement, reducing the apparent volume and overstating the density. For porous oxide ores, the measured density can be 10-20% higher than the actual in-situ density. Wax-coating before immersion seals the pores and gives a more accurate result. Calliper methods work for regular-geometry samples but may miss internal porosity. Gamma-gamma logging measures in-situ density and captures moisture effects. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of the density determination method, number of measurements, and consideration of porosity effects.
key_concept: Uncoated water immersion overstates density for porous ores — wax-coating or in-situ methods are required

question: Grade continuity in a resource estimate refers to:

options.A: Whether the company will continue mining at the same grade
options.B: Whether the grade exceeds the cut-off continuously
options.C: The degree to which grades can be predicted between sample points, reflecting the spatial correlation of mineralisation
options.D: The consistency of laboratory assay results over time

explanation: Grade continuity describes how predictable grades are between known data points. High grade continuity means that if two nearby samples show similar grades, the grade between them is likely to be similar — this supports higher resource classification. Low grade continuity (high nugget effect, erratic grades) means grades between samples are unpredictable, requiring closer drill spacing for the same level of confidence. Grade continuity is quantified through the variogram and is a primary factor in resource classification. The JORC Code (Table 1 Section 3) requires that the confidence in grade continuity be discussed and its implications for classification explained.
key_concept: Grade continuity = spatial predictability of grades between sample points, quantified by variograms

question: A Mineral Resource estimate includes dilution and recovery assumptions. Under JORC, is this appropriate?

options.A: Yes, but only for underground mining methods
options.B: No — neither Resources nor Reserves should include dilution
options.C: Yes, Resources should always include mining dilution and ore loss
options.D: No — Mineral Resources are estimated in-situ; dilution and recovery are Modifying Factors applied when converting to Ore Reserves

explanation: Mineral Resources are reported as in-situ estimates — they represent the mineralisation as it exists in the ground before any mining takes place. Mining dilution (inclusion of waste material) and ore loss (mineralised material that cannot be recovered) are Modifying Factors that are applied during the conversion of Resources to Ore Reserves (Table 1 Section 4). Including mining dilution or ore loss assumptions in a Resource estimate would conflate the geological model with mining assumptions, making it difficult for readers to assess either independently. However, the cut-off grade used for the Resource should consider the likely mining scenario at a conceptual level.
key_concept: Resources are in-situ estimates; dilution and recovery are Modifying Factors for Reserves

question: A deposit has 60% of its resource classified as Indicated and 40% as Inferred. A company is considering converting this to an Ore Reserve. What is the maximum percentage that could theoretically be converted?

options.A: 0% — a feasibility study must be completed first before any conversion
options.B: 40% — only the Inferred portion can be converted
options.C: 60% — only the Indicated portion can be converted (to Probable Reserve)
options.D: 100% — all resource categories can become Reserves

explanation: Under JORC (Clauses 28-29), only Indicated and Measured Resources can be converted to Ore Reserves — Measured to Proved Reserves and Indicated to Probable Reserves. Inferred Resources cannot be converted to any Reserve category because they lack sufficient geological confidence. Therefore, the maximum conversion is limited to the 60% Indicated portion. In practice, the actual conversion would typically be lower than 60% because not all Indicated material will be economically extractable after applying Modifying Factors (mining, processing, economic, and other considerations). The Inferred material is excluded from the Reserve entirely.
key_concept: Only Measured and Indicated Resources can convert to Reserves — Inferred cannot

question: The nearest-neighbour estimation method is generally considered the LEAST sophisticated estimation technique. Under what circumstances might its use be appropriate for a JORC-compliant resource?

options.A: When the Competent Person lacks access to kriging software
options.B: Only for coal deposits
options.C: It is never appropriate under JORC
options.D: For a global resource estimate check or validation of more sophisticated methods, or for deposits with very regular drill spacing and simple geology

explanation: Nearest-neighbour assigns each block the grade of the closest sample. While unsophisticated, it has legitimate uses: as a global validation check against kriging or IDW estimates (they should produce similar global averages), for deposits with very regular drill spacing where data clustering is not an issue, or for early-stage estimates where more complex methods are not yet justified. Table 1 Section 3 requires that the Competent Person justify the chosen method. Lack of software access is not an acceptable justification — the method must be appropriate for the deposit characteristics and the intended use of the estimate.
key_concept: Nearest-neighbour is valid for global checks or simple deposits but must be justified

question: When reporting a Mineral Resource, the JORC Code requires the Competent Person to consider 'all relevant factors.' Which of the following is NOT a factor that must be considered?

options.A: Cut-off grade assumptions and their basis
options.B: The geological model and estimation methodology
options.C: Environmental and social factors that may affect reasonable prospects for economic extraction
options.D: The company's current share price and market capitalisation

explanation: Table 1 Section 3 requires consideration of geological models, estimation methods, cut-off grades, density, classification, and environmental/social factors. The company's share price and market capitalisation are irrelevant to the technical and geological assessment of a Mineral Resource. The Resource estimate must be based on geological and technical merit, not on the company's market valuation. Including market factors would compromise the independence and objectivity of the Competent Person's assessment and violate the Competence principle.
key_concept: Resource estimation considers geological and technical factors, never market valuation

question: A resource model uses a search ellipse with a maximum range of 300m, but the drill spacing in parts of the deposit is 150m. What concern does this raise?

options.A: No concern — the search range should always exceed drill spacing
options.B: The search range should always be less than drill spacing
options.C: The search range is so much larger than the drill spacing that blocks estimated using distant samples may have low reliability, yet could still be classified as Indicated or Measured
options.D: The search range should be exactly equal to drill spacing

explanation: When the search range significantly exceeds the drill spacing, blocks at the margins of the data (or in gaps between holes) may be estimated using samples at considerable distance. These distant-informed blocks may have high kriging variance and poor estimation quality, but could appear to be well-estimated by simple metrics. This creates a risk of over-classification — blocks that should be Inferred being classified as Indicated. Best practice uses restricted search ranges for higher classification categories and requires minimum numbers of informing samples from multiple drill holes. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of search parameters and their relationship to data spacing.
key_concept: Excessive search ranges relative to drill spacing risk over-classification of resource blocks

question: A Competent Person is defining estimation domains for a polymetallic VMS deposit with distinct massive sulphide, stringer, and disseminated zones. What is the MOST important principle for domain definition?

options.A: Domains should be defined solely by the block model software's automatic clustering algorithm
options.B: Domains should be geologically meaningful, reflecting distinct populations with different grade distributions, and their boundaries should be supported by geological evidence such as lithology, alteration, or structural controls
options.C: A single domain should always be used to maximise sample numbers for variography
options.D: Domains should be defined purely by grade thresholds using a fixed cut-off

explanation: Estimation domains must be geologically meaningful and statistically distinct. Each domain should represent a population with internally consistent grade characteristics — combining massive sulphide (high grade, low variance) with disseminated mineralisation (low grade, high variance) into a single domain would violate stationarity assumptions and produce biased estimates. Domain boundaries should be justified by geological evidence: lithology contacts, alteration boundaries, structural controls, or mineralisation style. Grade-only thresholds risk creating artificial domains that do not reflect geological reality. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of the geological interpretation underpinning estimation domains and the rationale for their definition.
key_concept: Estimation domains must be geologically defined, statistically distinct populations

question: A deposit has been drilled on an irregular pattern — some areas at 25m spacing and others at 100m. The global mean grade of all composites is 2.1 g/t Au. A Competent Person suspects this naively calculated mean is biased. Why?

options.A: The mean is always unbiased if all samples are weighted equally
options.B: Wider-spaced samples are always less accurate than closely-spaced ones
options.C: The laboratory instruments drift over time, biasing later assays
options.D: Closely spaced drilling tends to be concentrated in higher-grade areas, so the clustered high-grade samples are over-represented in the global mean

explanation: Preferential or clustered sampling is one of the most common sources of bias in resource estimation. Companies tend to drill closely in areas where initial results are encouraging (higher grades), creating data clusters that over-represent high-grade zones when a simple arithmetic mean is calculated. This is called information bias or clustering bias. Declustering techniques — such as cell declustering or polygonal declustering — assign lower weights to clustered samples and higher weights to isolated samples to produce an unbiased global estimate. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of how data clustering has been addressed in the estimation.
key_concept: Clustered drilling in high-grade areas biases naive statistics — declustering is essential

question: Compositing in resource estimation involves combining individual assay intervals into equal-length composites. What is the PRIMARY reason for compositing?

options.A: To reduce the number of samples and speed up the estimation software
options.B: To artificially smooth out high grades
options.C: To eliminate the need for variography
options.D: To regularise sample support so that each data point represents an equal volume of rock, ensuring valid geostatistical analysis

explanation: Geostatistical methods assume data with equal support (volume). Raw assay intervals often vary in length (0.3m to 3m), meaning each sample represents a different volume of rock. Compositing regularises the data to a consistent length so that each composite has equal statistical weight. The composite length should reflect the likely selective mining unit or a practical interval related to the mineralisation style — typically 1-2m for gold deposits or matching bench heights for bulk mining. Compositing does produce some smoothing, but that is a secondary effect, not the primary purpose. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of the compositing method, length, and rationale.
key_concept: Compositing regularises sample support for valid geostatistical analysis

question: Multiple indicator kriging (MIK) is particularly suited to which type of deposit?

options.A: Deposits where no variogram can be fitted
options.B: Deposits with only 10-20 drill holes
options.C: Deposits with complex, highly skewed grade distributions where conventional kriging may over-smooth the grade-tonnage relationship
options.D: Deposits with simple, normally distributed grade populations

explanation: MIK estimates the full conditional cumulative distribution function (ccdf) of grade at each block, rather than just the mean grade. This is particularly valuable for deposits with highly skewed, multi-modal, or complex grade distributions — such as nuggety gold deposits — where ordinary kriging tends to over-smooth grades and produce unreliable grade-tonnage curves. MIK works by transforming the grade data into a series of indicator variables at multiple thresholds and kriging each indicator separately. The resulting grade-tonnage curves better represent the inherent variability of the deposit. However, MIK requires substantial data and careful implementation. Table 1 Section 3 requires justification of why MIK was selected over simpler methods.
key_concept: MIK estimates grade distributions for complex, skewed deposits where kriging over-smooths

question: A grade-tonnage curve for a gold deposit shows that at a 0.5 g/t cut-off the resource is 10 Mt at 2.0 g/t, but at 1.0 g/t cut-off it drops to 4 Mt at 3.5 g/t. What does this steep tonnage drop indicate?

options.A: The estimation method is unreliable
options.B: The variogram range is too short
options.C: The block model has too many blocks
options.D: A large proportion of the resource sits between 0.5 and 1.0 g/t — the deposit is grade-sensitive and small changes in cut-off grade dramatically affect the reported resource

explanation: Grade-tonnage curves reveal the sensitivity of the resource to cut-off grade assumptions. A steep drop in tonnage over a narrow grade range means a large volume of material exists near the marginal cut-off — the resource is highly sensitive to commodity price fluctuations. This is critical information for investors and for mine planning because small changes in gold price could render 60% of the resource sub-economic. Table 1 Section 3 effectively requires this sensitivity to be communicated by requiring discussion of the cut-off grade basis and the resource's sensitivity to key assumptions. A Competent Person should present grade-tonnage curves or tabulate the resource at multiple cut-offs to convey this risk.
key_concept: Grade-tonnage curves reveal resource sensitivity to cut-off grade and economic assumptions

question: Swath plots are a common validation tool for resource estimates. What do they compare?

options.A: The estimated block grades (averaged in slices or swaths along each axis) against the declustered composite grades in the same slices
options.B: The tonnage from open pit optimisation against underground stope optimisation
options.C: The drill hole collar coordinates against the block model extents
options.D: The variogram model against the gold price forecast

explanation: Swath plots compare the mean estimated block grade in successive slices (e.g., 50m-wide east-west swaths) against the mean declustered composite grade in the same slices, along each of the three principal axes (easting, northing, elevation). If the model is globally unbiased, the two curves should track closely. Systematic divergence indicates estimation bias — the model may be under- or over-estimating grades in specific parts of the deposit. Swath plots are one of several validation techniques (alongside visual inspection, global statistics comparison, and change-of-support checks) that Table 1 Section 3 expects a Competent Person to perform and disclose.
key_concept: Swath plots compare model grades against data grades in slices to detect spatial bias

question: What is 'change of support' in the context of resource estimation, and why does it matter?

options.A: Changing from one mining contractor to another during operations
options.B: Updating the resource estimate when new drill data becomes available
options.C: Converting from metric to imperial units in the block model
options.D: The statistical effect whereby the variance of grades decreases as the volume (support) over which grades are averaged increases — affecting the proportion of material above a given cut-off

explanation: Change of support is a fundamental geostatistical principle: as the averaging volume increases (from a 1m drill sample to a 10m mining block to a 50m panel), the variance of grades decreases and the grade distribution becomes tighter around the mean. This means the proportion of material above (or below) any cut-off grade changes with the block size used. A model with small blocks will show more material at extreme grades (both high and low) than one with large blocks. This has direct implications for recoverable resource estimation and selective mining unit (SMU) assumptions. Methods like MIK with change-of-support corrections or conditional simulation address this issue explicitly.
key_concept: Change of support: grade variance decreases with increasing block volume, affecting cut-off grade economics

question: A Competent Person must choose between ordinary kriging (OK) and conditional simulation (CS) for a deposit where understanding local grade variability and selectivity is critical for mine planning. Which statement is correct?

options.A: OK is always superior because it produces minimum-variance estimates
options.B: CS should be used because it reproduces the full range of grade variability including local uncertainty, while OK produces smoothed estimates that understate grade variability at the block scale
options.C: CS is prohibited under the JORC Code because it introduces randomness
options.D: OK and CS always produce identical results, so the choice does not matter

explanation: Ordinary kriging produces the best estimate of the mean grade for each block (minimum variance, unbiased) but smooths the grade distribution — it overstates tonnage near the mean grade and understates both high-grade and low-grade tails. Conditional simulation generates multiple equally probable realisations that honour the data values, histogram, and spatial correlation. Each realisation reproduces the full grade variability, and the ensemble of realisations quantifies local uncertainty. This makes CS particularly valuable when selectivity, dilution, or ore loss is critical. Both methods are acceptable under JORC; the Competent Person must justify the choice based on the deposit characteristics and the intended use of the estimate.
key_concept: Conditional simulation reproduces grade variability and quantifies uncertainty; kriging smooths

question: A deposit contains two adjacent mineralisation zones: a high-grade quartz vein and a lower-grade disseminated halo. The Competent Person must decide whether to use hard or soft boundaries between these domains. What is the key criterion?

options.A: If there is a sharp geological contact with a distinct grade break, hard boundaries are appropriate; if there is a gradational transition, soft boundaries that allow data from one domain to inform the other may be more appropriate
options.B: Soft boundaries should always be used to maximise the number of informing samples
options.C: Hard boundaries should always be used because they are simpler to implement
options.D: The choice depends entirely on which option produces the higher resource tonnage

explanation: The choice between hard and soft domain boundaries must be driven by the geological reality at the contact. Hard boundaries prevent data from one domain influencing estimates in the adjacent domain — appropriate where sharp geological contacts produce abrupt grade changes (e.g., a vein contact). Soft boundaries allow some cross-domain data influence — appropriate for gradational transitions such as disseminated halos around veins, or weathering profiles. Contact analysis (comparing grade profiles across the boundary) should be used to determine the appropriate approach. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of the domain boundary treatment and its geological justification. Using hard boundaries where grade transitions are gradational can create artificial grade discontinuities in the model.
key_concept: Hard vs soft domain boundaries must reflect the geological nature of the contact

question: Beyond simple top-cutting, what is an alternative outlier management strategy that a Competent Person might employ for a gold deposit with extreme high grades?

options.A: Assign all high-grade samples to a separate resource category called 'Speculative'
options.B: Restrict the distance of influence of high-grade composites by using a reduced search neighbourhood for samples above a specified threshold
options.C: Report the resource without any outlier treatment and let the market decide
options.D: Delete all samples above the 95th percentile from the dataset

explanation: Restricting the search distance for high-grade composites is a sophisticated alternative to simple top-cutting. Rather than permanently reducing or capping high grades, this approach limits how far each high-grade sample can influence surrounding blocks. A high-grade intersection can still contribute its full value to nearby blocks (where it is geologically reasonable) but cannot dominate estimates at greater distances where its influence is uncertain. Other advanced techniques include high-grade domaining (treating high-grade zones as separate estimation domains) and indicator-based methods. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of how extreme grades have been managed and the impact on the estimate. Deleting samples is never appropriate as it discards real geological information.
key_concept: High-grade search restriction limits outlier influence spatially rather than reducing grade values

question: In a sub-blocked model, parent cells are subdivided at domain boundaries or topographic surfaces. What is the critical rule regarding grade estimation in sub-blocked models?

options.A: Sub-cells always receive a grade of zero to be conservative
options.B: Grade is typically estimated at the parent cell level, and all sub-cells within the same domain inherit the parent cell grade — this avoids artificial grade variability from support effects
options.C: Each sub-cell must be estimated independently using its own search parameters
options.D: Sub-cells must have higher grades than parent cells to compensate for their smaller volume

explanation: In sub-blocked (or sub-celled) models, parent cells are divided into smaller sub-cells to honour geological boundaries, pit shells, or topography with greater resolution. However, grade estimation should be performed at the parent cell level because the parent cell size is chosen to match the data spacing and provide reliable estimates. All sub-cells within the same domain inherit the parent cell grade. Estimating grades directly into sub-cells would produce estimates at a support smaller than justified by the data density, leading to artificial grade variability and unreliable local estimates. The sub-blocking provides volumetric accuracy at boundaries while the parent-cell estimation provides grade accuracy. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of block and sub-block dimensions.
key_concept: Sub-cells inherit parent cell grades to maintain estimation reliability at appropriate support

question: A Competent Person is selecting an estimation method for a large, low-grade porphyry copper deposit with regular 100m x 100m drill spacing. Which method is MOST appropriate and why?

options.A: Multiple indicator kriging — because all deposits require MIK
options.B: Ordinary kriging — the deposit's large scale, relatively low grade variability, and regular data spacing make it well suited to OK, which will produce reliable block estimates with quantified estimation variance
options.C: Polygonal estimation — because porphyry deposits always use polygonal methods
options.D: Nearest-neighbour — because the drill spacing is too wide for geostatistics

explanation: Porphyry copper deposits are typically large, with relatively continuous grade distributions and moderate variability — characteristics well-suited to ordinary kriging. The regular drill spacing enables reliable variogram modelling, and the deposit's scale and continuity mean OK can produce robust block estimates with quantifiable estimation variance. MIK would be unnecessarily complex for a grade distribution that is not highly skewed. Nearest-neighbour and polygonal methods, while valid as global checks, would not leverage the spatial correlation structure that OK captures. The JORC Code does not prescribe a specific estimation method; the Competent Person must justify the method's appropriateness for the deposit type, data density, and grade characteristics (Table 1 Section 3).
key_concept: Estimation method selection must match deposit characteristics — simple deposits suit simpler methods

question: When should a block model be rotated (i.e., have its axes aligned to something other than the grid north/east/elevation)?

options.A: Rotation is only applied to underground deposits
options.B: When the orebody geometry, strike direction, or principal directions of grade continuity are oblique to the standard grid, rotating the model to align with geological controls improves estimation accuracy and volumetric representation
options.C: Only when the mining lease boundary is not aligned with north
options.D: Block models should never be rotated — they must always align with mine grid north

explanation: Block model rotation aligns the model axes with the dominant geological orientation of the deposit — typically along strike, across strike, and down dip. This is important because: (1) blocks aligned with the orebody minimise the volume of partially-filled boundary blocks, improving tonnage accuracy; (2) the estimation search can more naturally follow the geological continuity; and (3) grade estimation within oriented blocks better represents the selective mining geometry. For steeply dipping or obliquely striking orebodies, a non-rotated model can produce significant volumetric errors at domain boundaries. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of block model orientation and its geological rationale.
key_concept: Block model rotation aligns estimation with geological controls for accuracy

question: A narrow vein gold deposit has a true width of 1.5m but the company reports the resource using 10m x 10m x 5m blocks. What is the problem with this approach?

options.A: Smaller blocks would produce a lower tonnage
options.B: There is no problem — larger blocks are always more reliable
options.C: The block size far exceeds the minimum mining width, resulting in excessive internal dilution within each block and producing an unrealistically low average grade that does not represent what could actually be selectively mined
options.D: The JORC Code mandates 1m x 1m x 1m blocks for all vein deposits

explanation: For narrow vein deposits, the block size must reflect the minimum mining width (MMW) — the narrowest width that can be practically mined. Using 10m blocks for a 1.5m vein means each block averages the vein grade with ~8.5m of waste, producing unrealistically diluted grades that do not represent the material that a selective mining operation would recover. The resource would be understated in grade and overstated in tonnage relative to what could actually be mined. Best practice for narrow veins uses block widths close to the MMW (e.g., 1.5-3m) or reports the resource on a vein-width basis with separate internal dilution calculations. Table 1 Section 3 requires that block size be appropriate for the deposit and mining scenario.
key_concept: Block size must relate to minimum mining width — oversized blocks dilute narrow vein grades

question: A Competent Person presents a resource estimate with a sensitivity analysis showing the resource at three different gold prices (+20%, base case, -20%). Why is this sensitivity analysis important under JORC?

options.A: It demonstrates how the resource tonnage and grade change with cut-off grade assumptions linked to commodity price, allowing readers to assess the robustness of the 'reasonable prospects for eventual economic extraction' determination
options.B: It is a purely academic exercise with no practical relevance
options.C: It replaces the need for a feasibility study
options.D: It is required only for deposits in developing countries

explanation: Resource sensitivity analysis demonstrates how robust the estimate is to changes in key assumptions, particularly commodity price (which drives cut-off grade). A resource that drops by 70% with a 20% price decline is far more risky than one that drops by only 10%. This information is material for investors and for the Competent Person's own assessment of 'reasonable prospects for eventual economic extraction' (Clause 20). Table 1 Section 3 encourages transparency about the sensitivity of the estimate to input parameters. Best practice includes sensitivity to cut-off grade, commodity price, density assumptions, and high-grade management. Sensitivity analysis does not replace a feasibility study but informs risk assessment.
key_concept: Sensitivity analysis reveals resource robustness to commodity price and cut-off grade changes

question: A dataset contains 15% of assay results reported as below the detection limit (BDL) for copper at <0.01%. How should these BDL samples be treated in the resource estimate?

options.A: Assign them a value of half the detection limit (0.005%) — this is the most common approach for moderate proportions of BDL data, and the treatment must be disclosed
options.B: Exclude all BDL samples from the dataset entirely
options.C: Assign them the mean grade of the entire deposit
options.D: Assign them a value of zero, regardless of the proportion of BDL data

explanation: Below-detection-limit (BDL) samples represent real geological information — the grade is low but not necessarily zero. The most common treatment is to assign half the detection limit value, which assumes a uniform distribution between zero and the detection limit. This is generally appropriate when BDL samples represent a moderate proportion of the data (<20-30%). For higher proportions, more sophisticated approaches (e.g., maximum likelihood estimation or indicator methods) may be needed. Excluding BDL samples would bias the mean grade upward by removing low-grade data. Assigning zero underestimates the contained metal. Table 1 Section 3 requires disclosure of how BDL samples were treated and the proportion of data affected.
key_concept: Below-detection-limit assays are typically assigned half the detection limit; treatment must be disclosed

question: A Competent Person discovers that the geological domaining used in a previous resource estimate grouped two lithologically distinct units into a single domain. Contact plots show a sharp grade discontinuity at the lithological boundary. What is the likely impact of this error?

options.A: The estimate will be biased because high-grade samples from one unit will have influenced (contaminated) block grades in the adjacent lower-grade unit, and vice versa, producing overestimation in the low-grade unit and underestimation in the high-grade unit
options.B: The estimate will have higher nugget effect but the same mean grade
options.C: The tonnage will be correct but reported in the wrong classification category
options.D: No impact — domaining errors are cosmetic and do not affect grade estimates

explanation: Incorrect geological domaining is one of the most consequential errors in resource estimation. When two distinct populations are combined, the estimation algorithm treats all data as coming from a single statistical population. High-grade samples from one unit will be used to estimate blocks in the lower-grade unit near the contact, and vice versa. This produces systematic grade smearing across the boundary — the low-grade unit is overestimated and the high-grade unit is underestimated. The global mean may appear correct, but local block estimates near the contact will be significantly biased. The variogram will also be poorly modelled because it attempts to describe the spatial structure of a mixed population. This is why Table 1 Section 3 places such emphasis on the geological model underpinning the estimate.
key_concept: Incorrect domaining causes grade smearing across boundaries — one of the most impactful estimation errors

question: When reporting a Mineral Resource, JORC requires the resource to be stated as tonnes and grade. A company additionally reports 'contained metal' (e.g., 500,000 oz Au). What must the Competent Person ensure regarding contained metal reporting?

options.A: Contained metal may be reported but must be accompanied by the tonnage and grade from which it is derived, must not be reported with false precision, and must clearly distinguish between resource categories (Measured, Indicated, Inferred) — it must not imply that all the metal will be recovered
options.B: Contained metal is only permitted for gold deposits
options.C: Contained metal can be reported as a single combined figure across all resource categories
options.D: Contained metal should never be reported as it is misleading

explanation: Contained metal (tonnes x grade x conversion factor) is a useful summary metric but carries risks of misinterpretation. The JORC Code requires that contained metal always be accompanied by the underlying tonnage and grade, reported separately for each resource category (Measured, Indicated, Inferred). Combining contained metal across categories obscures the different confidence levels — 100,000 oz in the Measured category is far more reliable than 100,000 oz in the Inferred category. Additionally, contained metal must not be presented with precision that exceeds the estimate's accuracy (e.g., reporting to single ounces when the estimate has +/-20% uncertainty). Clause 25 and Table 1 Section 3 govern the appropriate format for resource reporting, emphasising transparency and the avoidance of material misrepresentation.
key_concept: Contained metal must accompany tonnes and grade, be reported by category, and not imply full recovery
